name: Classroom Market Day: Supply and Demand in Action
description: Set up a classroom marketplace where students produce goods (drawings, origami, friendship bracelets, bookmarks) and trade them using token currency. Each round changes conditions: in round 1, everyone produces freely and trades; in round 2, the supply of one popular item is limited; in round 3, more buyers enter the market. After each round, record prices and discuss what happened. Graph price changes across rounds. Identify the forces of supply and demand in action. Debrief: why did prices change? What happened when a popular item became scarce? What happens when nobody wants a product?
learning_objectives: Observe how prices emerge from the interaction of buyers and sellers
Understand that scarcity drives prices up and surplus drives prices down
Experience the difference between barter and money-based exchange
Graph and analyze price data across changing market conditions
variations: Foundation (K-2): Simple trading circle with 3-4 items; focus on fair vs. unfair trades
High School: Add production costs, profit calculations, and market manipulation scenarios

name: Barter vs. Money: Trading Game
description: Each student receives cards representing different goods (apples, fish, shoes, bread, tools). In round 1, students must barter to get what they need -- but they can only trade directly (apples for shoes, not apples for fish for shoes). Experience the frustration of the 'double coincidence of wants' problem. In round 2, introduce money tokens that everyone accepts. Feel the difference. Discuss: why was bartering so hard? What makes money useful? Why does every society eventually create some form of currency? What happens if everyone has too much money?
learning_objectives: Experience the 'double coincidence of wants' problem in barter systems
Understand why money exists as a medium of exchange
Recognize that money solves a fundamental coordination problem
Describe the difference between direct trade and money-mediated trade
variations: Foundation (PreK-K): Use toy food and household items for physical trading practice
Middle School: Add complexity with perishable vs. durable goods and discuss store of value

name: Lemonade Stand Business Plan
description: Design a complete business plan for a lemonade stand. Decide: What product will you sell (just lemonade, or also cookies and snacks)? What will you charge? What are your startup costs (supplies, cups, sign, table)? Where will you set up (location analysis)? Who are your target customers? How will you market your stand? Calculate your break-even point: how many cups must you sell to cover costs? Choose a business structure (sole proprietorship or partnership with a friend) and explain why. Present your plan to classmate 'investors' who ask tough questions and decide whether to fund your venture.
learning_objectives: Create a basic business plan with product, market, costs, and revenue model
Calculate startup costs and break-even point
Choose a business structure and justify the choice
Present a business idea persuasively and respond to critical questions
variations: Foundation (K-2): Run a pretend store in the classroom with play money and simple pricing
High School: Add financial projections, competitor analysis, and legal requirements for a real small business

name: Compare Business Types: Research and Present
description: Research three real businesses: one sole proprietorship or small local business, one corporation (publicly traded company), and one nonprofit organization. For each business, answer: Who owns it? Who makes the key decisions? What happens if the business loses money or gets sued? How does it raise money to operate? Who benefits from its success? Create a comparison chart showing the differences across all three structures. Present your findings to the class. Discuss: why would someone choose each structure? What are the tradeoffs between simplicity, protection, and control?
materials: list of suggested businesses to research
learning_objectives: Compare ownership, liability, and governance across business structures
Research real businesses and extract organizational information
Understand why different situations call for different business structures
Present research findings clearly with evidence and analysis
variations: Elementary (3-5): Visit or research three local businesses and draw pictures showing who works there and what they do
College: Add LLC and cooperative analysis with tax implications and governance documents

name: Personal Budget Challenge: Track Spending for a Week
description: Track every dollar you spend (or that is spent on your behalf) for one full week. Categorize each expense as a need (food, shelter, transportation, school supplies) or a want (entertainment, snacks, decorations). At the end of the week, total each category. Create a monthly budget based on a realistic income for your area: if you were living on your own with a job paying the local minimum wage, could you cover your needs? What would you cut? What would you keep? Present your budget and explain your priorities. Discuss: what surprised you about your spending? What would you change?
materials: average rent and utility costs for the area
learning_objectives: Track and categorize personal spending accurately
Distinguish between needs and wants in spending decisions
Create a realistic monthly budget with income and expense categories
Make and justify tradeoff decisions when income is limited
variations: Elementary (3-4): Track spending of play money allowance over a week of classroom activities
College: Add taxes, insurance, retirement savings, and debt repayment to the budget

name: Read a Simple Income Statement
description: Given a simplified income statement for a fictional small bakery, answer a series of increasingly complex questions. Level 1: How much total revenue did the bakery earn? What were the biggest expenses? Was the bakery profitable? Level 2: What is the profit margin? If the owner wanted to increase profit by 20%, what could they change? Level 3: Compare two months side by side. What changed? Why might revenue have increased but profit decreased? Write a one-paragraph analysis advising the bakery owner on what to do next, supported by specific numbers from the statement.
materials: question worksheet with three difficulty levels
learning_objectives: Read and extract information from a basic income statement
Calculate profit and profit margin from revenue and expense data
Analyze changes between time periods in financial data
Write a data-supported recommendation based on financial analysis
variations: Elementary (3-5): Use a pictograph version showing money coming in and going out of a lemonade stand
High School: Use a real publicly traded company's income statement from SEC filings

name: Draft a Simple Contract Between Friends
description: Two students agree on a trade or service arrangement. Examples: one student will create artwork for the other in exchange for help with a school project; one student will lend a book for two weeks in exchange for borrowing a game. Write out the agreement as a formal contract, including: who the parties are, what each person agrees to do, by when, what happens if one person does not follow through, and how disputes will be resolved. Exchange contracts with another pair for review: did they miss any important elements? Is anything ambiguous? Revise based on feedback. Discuss: what makes this agreement enforceable? What if there is no written record?
learning_objectives: Identify and include essential contract elements: parties, offer, acceptance, consideration
Write clear terms that both parties can understand and agree to
Recognize ambiguity in agreements and revise for clarity
Understand that written agreements are stronger than verbal ones
variations: Foundation (K-2): Draw a picture agreement showing what each person will do and sign it
High School: Draft a real freelance service agreement and compare it to professional templates

name: Mock Trial: Breach of Contract
description: Stage a simplified trial based on a breach of contract scenario: a student hired a classmate to build a website for their small business for $500. The website was delivered late, missing features that were promised, and the buyer refuses to pay the full amount. The builder says the requirements were unclear and they did extra work not in the original agreement. Assign roles: plaintiff (buyer), defendant (builder), attorneys for each side, judge, and jury. Each side presents opening statements, evidence (the original written agreement, emails, the delivered website), and closing arguments. The jury deliberates and delivers a verdict. Debrief: what made the arguments persuasive? Was the contract clear enough? What would have prevented this dispute?
materials: case scenario handout with background facts
learning_objectives: Understand how contract disputes are resolved in court
Evaluate evidence and arguments from multiple perspectives
Recognize that clear contract terms prevent disputes
Experience the roles of plaintiff, defendant, judge, and jury
variations: Elementary (3-5): Simplified dispute resolution circle where students take turns being the 'judge' for playground disagreements
High School: Use a real contract law case and apply legal standards for breach, damages, and remedies

name: Ethics Dilemma Discussion: Business Scenarios
description: Present three real-world business ethics dilemmas for structured discussion. Scenario 1: A company discovers its best-selling product has a minor safety flaw that has not hurt anyone yet. A recall would cost millions. Do they recall or wait? Scenario 2: A manager is asked to hire the CEO's unqualified nephew for a job that better candidates applied for. What should they do? Scenario 3: An algorithm used for hiring is twice as likely to reject applicants from certain zip codes. The algorithm is more profitable than human reviewers. Should the company keep using it? For each scenario, students identify stakeholders, list options, evaluate consequences, and defend a position. There are no easy answers -- the point is the quality of reasoning, not the conclusion.
learning_objectives: Identify stakeholders affected by business decisions
Evaluate multiple courses of action and their consequences
Defend an ethical position with reasoned arguments
Recognize that ethical dilemmas often have no clearly right answer
variations: Elementary (3-4): Simpler scenarios: 'Your friend's toy broke at your house. Do you tell them or hide it?'
College: Apply formal ethical frameworks (utilitarian, deontological, virtue ethics) to each scenario

name: Stakeholder Map for a Local Business
description: Choose a real local business that students are familiar with (a restaurant, grocery store, gym, or school). Create a comprehensive stakeholder map identifying every group affected by the business: owners, employees, customers, suppliers, neighbors, local government, competitors, the environment, and the broader community. For each stakeholder, describe: what do they want from the business? How are they affected by its decisions? What power do they have? Draw the map as a visual diagram showing relationships and influence. Then analyze a specific decision the business might face (raising prices, expanding hours, reducing packaging waste) and trace how it affects each stakeholder differently. Discuss: whose interests should come first? Can everyone win?
materials: large paper or whiteboard for mapping
learning_objectives: Identify all stakeholders affected by a business, not just owners and customers
Understand that business decisions create winners and losers
Analyze how a specific decision ripples through a stakeholder network
Recognize that responsible business requires considering multiple perspectives
variations: Elementary (3-4): Map the stakeholders of the school cafeteria using pictures and simple labels
College: Add quantitative stakeholder analysis with power-interest matrices and ESG frameworks
